statement: I can describe sets using set builder notation.
I can perform algebraic arithmetic on vectors in a Euclidean vector space R^n (e.g., addition, scalar multiplication, norm, and dot product).
I can represent vectors arithmetic geometrically for vectors in R^2 and R^3.
I can decide whether given vectors are parallel.
I can decide whether given vectors are perpendicular.
I can compute the angle between any two vectors.
I can represent a line in R^2 and R^3 in vector form.
I can represent a line in R^2 and R^3 in parametric form.
Given an algebraic description of a line in R^2 or R^3, I can visualize the line.
I can represent a plane in R^3 given a normal vector and a point.
Given an algebraic description of a plane, I can visualize the plane.
I can perform row reduction on any matrix and reduce it to REF and RREF.
I can determine when a matrix is in REF or RREF form.
Given an augmented matrix of a linear system, I can find the general solution to the system.
Given the general solution to a system of linear equations, I can find particular solutions.
Given the REF of an augmented matrix, I can determine the type of solution set the system has (no solution, a unique solution, infinitely many solutions).
I can identify the solution type of a system based on information about the rank of its augmented and coefficient matrices.
Given a system of linear equations in R^2 or R^3, I can visualize the system and its general solution.
Given the matrix form A x = b where b is a vector in R^2 or R^3, I can visualize a particular solution for the system by writing b as a linear combination of the columns of A.
I can visualize the linear combination of vectors in R^2 and R^3.
I can interpret matrix-vector multiplication in terms of a linear combination of the columns of the matrix.
I can interpret matrix-vector multiplication using dot products.
I can add matrices of the same size, multiply a matrix by a scalar and I can multiply a matrix by a vector if the dimensions match up.
I can describe the general solution of a system parametrically and as a linear combination of vectors using correct mathematical notation.
I can describe a system in matrix-vector multiplication notation.
Given a linear transformation in any form, I can determine its domain and codomain.
Given a function from R^m to R^n I can apply the definition of a linear transformation or the Matrices theorem to decide whether it is a linear map.
Given a linear transformation from R^m to R^n I can compute its standard matrix by computing the output of the standard vectors.
Given a linear transformation T in any form I can find T(x) for any vector x.
Given a linear transformation with domain and codomain in R^2 or R^3 I can visualize the effect of the transformation on the unit square or the unit cube.
I can find (not memorize) the standard matrix representation of the geometric transformations (rotation, reflection, projection, shear, scaling) on R^2.
I can visualize the effect of the geometric linear transformations on R^2 and R^3.
Given two linear transformations T and S, in any form, I can compute the standard matrix of T o S or S o T or show why they are not defined.
I can visualize the effect of the composition of two linear transformations on a given vector in R^2 and R^3.
Given matrices A and B, I can compute AB, if defined, by applying A to columns of B using the matrix-vector multiplication.
Given matrices A and B, I can compute AB, if defined, by finding the ij-th entry of AB directly by computing the dot product between the ith row of A and the jth column of B.
I know the algebraic properties of matrix arithmetic.
Given a linear transformation T and its standard matrix A, I can perform row reduction on A to decide whether T is invertible.
Given a linear transformation T and its standard matrix A, if T is invertible, I can find its inverse map T^-1, and the standard matrix representation of T^-1, denoted by A^-1.
Given a linear transformation T:R^m-> R^n I can decide whether T is injective, surjective, and/or invertible or bijective.
I can visualize all the subspaces of R, R^2, and R^3.
Given a subset of R^n, I can verify whether it is a subspace or not.
Given a set of vectors v_1, ..., v_n in R, R^2 or R^3 I can visualize span(v_1, ..., v_n).
Given a set of vectors v_1, ..., v_k in R^n I can describe span(v_1, ..., v_k) and decide whether any given vector is in it or not.
I know the definition of the kernel and image of a linear transformation T and can decide if a given vector is in ker (T) or im (T), and can find vectors in ker (T) and im (T).
Given a linear transformation T, I can describe im (T) in set builder notation.
Given a linear transformation T, I can describe the ker (T) and im (T) as a span of vectors.
Given a linear transformation T with domain or codomain R, R^2, R^3 I can visualize ker (T) and im (T).
Given a linear transformation T(x)=Ax, I can connect ker (T) and im (T) to the solutions to Ax=b, for suitable choices of b.
Given a linear transformation T, I can connect the notion of injective/surjective to ker (T)/im (T).
I can determine if a given a set of vectors is linearly independent.
Given a subspace, I can determine a basis.
Given a subspace, I can determine its dimension.
Given a set of vectors, I can reduce it to a linearly independent set.
Given a set of vectors, I can visualize if a vector is redundant.
Given a linear transformation T(x)=Ax, I can connect ker (T) with the linear independence of columns of A and im(T) with the span of the columns of A.
I can connect the dimension of the kernel of a linear transformation to the dimension of its image through Rank-Nullity Theorem.
Given a subspace W of R^n described via a basis, I can construct other bases for W.
I can take any vector v in any R^n, take a basis B for R^n, and find the B coordinates of v in R^n.

Given any two bases B_1, B_2 of R^n, I can construct a matrix (change of basis matrix from B_1 to B_2) that take the B_1 coordinates of v as an input and compute the B_2 coordinates of v as an output.
Given a vector v in R^n (n=1, 2 or 3) and bases B_1, B_2 of R^n, I can visualize [v]_B_1 and [v]_B_2.
Given a linear transformation T:R^n-> R^n, and a basis B for R^n, I can compute the matrix of T with respect to B and use it to find output of vectors.
I can connect the matrix representation of a linear transformation T with respect to two different bases, using a change of basis matrix.
Given a geometric transformation T in R^2, I can find a basis B (assuming it exists), such that T with respect to B is diagonal.
Given a basis B such that a linear transformation T is diagonal, I can visualize T
Given a set of vectors, I can determine if they are orthogonal, orthonormal, or neither.
Given a subspace V, I can describe the orthogonal complement of V.
Given a vector x in R^n and a subspace V subset of R^n, I can decompose x into two components, one in V and one in the orthogonal complement of V.
Given a vector x in R^n and an orthonormal basis U, I can find [x]_U, the coordinates of x with respect to U without row reduction.
I can visualize the kernel and image of the orthogonal projection onto a subspace V of R^2, R^3.
Given a basis B for a subspace V, I can apply the Gram-Schmidt process to transform B into an orthonormal basis of V.
I can recognize if a linear transformation is orthogonal.
I can visualize the effect of an orthogonal linear transformation on the unit square/cube.
I understand computational properties of transposes.
Given an inconsistent system A x = b, I can find x* such that Ax* is closest to b.
I can connect projections onto a subspace to fitting a line to a set of data points.

I can visualize the absolute value of the determinant of a 2 x 2 and determinant of a 3 x 3 matrix as a factor by which the corresponding linear transformation changes the area of the unit square or the volume of the unit cube, respectively.
I can interpret the sign of the determinant of a matrix geometrically.
I can compute the determinant of an arbitrary square matrix.
I can use the fact that the determinant is multilinear in rows and columns to simplify/compute the determinant of a matrix.
Given square matrices A and B, and a scalar k, I can relate the det (AB), det (A^T), det (kA) and det (A^-1) to det A and det B.
I can use determinant to determine whether a given linear transformation is invertible.
Given a 2 x 2 matrix A, I can compute the inverse of A using the matrix inverse formula.
I can use row reduction or multiplication by elementary matrices to compute the determinant of a matrix.
Given a linear transformation T(x) = A x, I can connect the diagonalizability of A to the existence of an eigenbasis for T.
Given a geometric transformation in R^2, I can visualize its eigenvectors and their corresponding eigenvalues.
Given a linear transformation T(x), I can verify whether a vector v is an eigenvectors and if so, determine the corresponding eigenvalues.
Given a linear transformation A x, I can find its eigenvalues and their algebraic multiplicity using the characteristic polynomial.
Given a characteristic polynomial of a 2 x 2 matrix, I can determine determinant and trace of A.
Given a linear transformation T(x) and eigenvalue lambda, I can find a basis for the eigenspace corresponding to lambda and its geometric multiplicity.
Given a linear transformation in R^2, R^3, I can visualize all the eigenspaces of T.
Given a linear transformation T, I can state a condition regarding the diagonalizability of T, in terms of the algebraic and geometric multiplicities of its eigenvalues.
I can diagonalize a matrix A, if possible, by finding a change of basis matrix C and a diagonal matrix D such that A=CDC^-1.
Given a diagonalizable A, I can predict long term behavior of a dynamical system whose transition matrix is A
Given a symmetric matrix A, I can orthogonally diagonalize A (or orthogonally diagonalize the linear map whose standard matrix is A).
Given a symmetric matrix A, I can visualize the eigenspaces of A
I can correctly use mathematical notation.
I can correctly and effectively communicate ideas in mathematical language.
Given a statement I can decide whether it is true or false, and correctly justify my decision.
Given a description of a mathematical object I can find an explicit example that satisfies that definition.
